mnemonics.title: Coffman Conditions
mnemonics.content: My Hungry Neighbor Can't Wait (M-Mutual Exclusion, H-Hold & Wait, N-No Preemption, C-Circular Wait)

mnemonics.title: Deadlock Strategies
mnemonics.content: PAD: Prevention (eliminate conditions), Avoidance (Banker's Algorithm), Detection/Recovery (identify & resolve)

mnemonics.title: Banker's Algorithm Steps
mnemonics.content: RAP: Request <= Available? → Allocate Pretend → Perform Safety Check

mnemonics.title: Deadlock Detection Steps
mnemonics.content: LOCK: Look for cycles → Order processes → Check wait-for graph → Kill victim process

mnemonics.title: Recovery Criteria
mnemonics.content: LIRA: Least Impact process → Inspect Resource Amount → Recent Age priority

acronyms.usage: Visual tool for deadlock detection using circles/boxes

visualTips.visualization: Draw processes as circles & resources as squares. Arrows show requests/allocations. Deadlock exists if there's a closed loop.
Imagine a spreadsheet tracking 'Max Needs', 'Allocated', and 'Available' columns. Green highlight = safe state.
Picture processes as nodes with arrows pointing to who they're waiting for. Cycle = deadlock spider web.
Think of a Jenga tower - remove the block (process) causing least structural damage (system impact).

analogies.analogy: Traffic gridlock where 4 cars each wait for the next to move at a 4-way intersection.
Cautious bank manager who only approves loans if enough cash remains to cover all clients' potential withdrawals.
Fire alarm triggered after smoke clears - detection system 'sees' danger that no longer exists.
Repossession agent taking back a car (resource) from one borrower to give it to another needing it more urgently.
